# K-12: Prioritize Emails with Codex

<!-- vimeo: 1197917784 | track: English (auto-generated) -->

Teachers spend a lot of time digging through email, especially when the inbox mixes parent concerns, student questions, school logistics, newsletters, and deadline reminders. In this demo, I'm using Ms. Rivera's teacher inbox. The scenario is simple. Ms. Rivera is a seventh-grade teacher with about 10 minutes before her next class, and she's looking to know what actually needs her attention. Next, Ms. Rivera turns to Codex to help. She tells it she's a teacher with only a few minutes between classes. She wants it to prioritize messages that are impacting today's classes, those items affecting students completing their assignments on time, and any messages coming from a parent with a concern. Ms. Rivera is asking Codex to go through the messages and create a triage table. She's not asking to write every response yet. She wants to find the important information and then group the inbox by priority. Reviewing the responses, Ms. Rivera sees how it's useful for her. Codex is looking across the emails and pulling out what matters. Students are blocked by a broken assignment link, parents are writing in that require a careful reply, admins are indicating deadlines that teachers need to follow, and there are certain items that are also impacting tomorrow's classes. This indicates what's urgent today that needs action, the section where something needs a reply soon, and then separates out those items that are just FYI or lower priority. Now Ms. Rivera is able to steer the results to make it even more useful for herself. She asks Codex to revisit this in a teacher-friendly end-of-day plan she can review. Anything affecting tomorrow's class right at the top, parent replies separated out, every deadline into a checklist, and then even short reply starters to get the messages going to the parents that need the most care. Ms. Rivera reviews the new plan. She sees the top priorities, understands which parents she needs to reply to, has quick sentence starters to get those replies going, and then figures out which students require her attention first. She's able to take quick action within a few minutes and knows where she needs to spend her time that's so precious. This is a reusable workflow. Teachers can tell Codex exactly what to inspect within their inbox, what's particularly urgent in their context, and then ask it to extract priorities, understand deadlines, and create an action list before they start replying. Codex is not here to replace the teacher's judgment. It gives you a clear starting point so less time is spent digging through email and more time goes through the messages that need care.
